verdicts.note: S1 and S4 fully support +2 high (program-expansion platform plus Appropriations vice-chair and enacted free-meals bill). S3 (Right to Repair) is not spending evidence and could be dropped from the citation list, but the score stands without it.

Rests entirely on a single Sierra Club endorsement [S6]; the basis itself concedes no climate statements exist in the dossier. One endorsement is not a 'strong endorsement pattern' under the guide's medium bar — this is indirect inference, so low.
Two independent signals (official page bill framing [S2] plus OneAmerica Votes endorsement [S1]) justify medium at modest +1.
Single pamphlet phrase (Office of Equity) — medium is the correct ceiling and +1 the correct magnitude; anything stronger would be unsupported.

Right to Repair is enacted record, but the rubric's tech axis concerns AI, data centers, and platform regulation; a consumer-electronics repair law says nothing about any of those. Mapping it here is an indirect inference — direction plausible, confidence low.
Genuinely mixed evidence in S2 (co-response teams vs accountability-framed encampment policy); 0 is the honest reading.

S2 shows one website plank opposing specifically UNFUNDED mandates plus a general cities-know-best line. Opposing unfunded mandates is a mainstream municipal position, not wholesale rejection of state standards; -2 (fully pole_a) is exaggerated for this evidence.

The S1 quote ('endless rules... increase government control') is about regulation, not spending levels, audits, or budgets; the basis itself admits no budget proposals exist. The spending direction is inferred from general small-government rhetoric — indirect inference, so low.

Term-limits pledge documented in S4 plus anti-career-politician framing in S1; +1 medium is correctly calibrated. Minor: the fundraising highlight bullet is context rather than a position, though factual per S3.
